summary: Three topics. First: the shewbread loaf-count debate concludes with Abaye's Rabbanan reading and Rava's Rabbi reading both presented; an unresolved contradiction remains. Second: the seven-day KG preparation begins - elders of Beit Din hand the KG to priestly elders who read the service to him daily and parade animals before him for identification (excluding goats). Third: Second Temple-era KG appointment corruption (Maratha bat Baytus bribes Agrippa for Yehoshua ben Gamla's appointment). Pre-Yom Kippur dietary restrictions for the KG are established: foods causing seminal emission (fine flour, eggs) are restricted; the KG is fed appropriately to prevent both hunger and emission.

display.whats: The Shewbread loaf-count debate concludes. Abaye reads the mishna as consistently Rabbanan (the KG gets proportionally less than half), while Rava reads the whole mishna as Rabbi's view (KG always takes half). A remaining contradiction about whether the KG always gets five loaves is left unresolved. The page then shifts to the new mishna: the elders of the Beit Din hand the KG over to elders of the priesthood for the week of preparation before Yom Kippur.
display.hint: The Shewbread count is resolved by showing the mishna's 'four or five' is explained by mishmar configurations. The transition to the seven-day preparation is a major topic shift - we are now setting up Yom Kippur itself.

learning.summary: The Shewbread debate ends: Abaye's Rabbanan reading and Rava's Rabbi reading are both defended. An unresolved contradiction remains about the loaf count. The mishna then begins the Yom Kippur preparation sequence: the elders of the Sanhedrin hand the KG over to elders of the priesthood, who instruct him in the Yom Kippur service for seven days. They also parade animals before him so he can recognize the Yom Kippur goats by sight.
learning.ahaMoment: The handoff between the Sanhedrin elders and the priestly elders is a formal transfer of jurisdiction - the Sanhedrin ensure the KG knows the law; the priestly elders ensure he can perform it.
learning.memoryAnchor: Shewbread count: Abaye = Rabbanan (proportional), Rava = Rabbi (always half). Then: elders hand off KG to priests for seven days of Yom Kippur preparation.
learning.openQuestion: Why do the priestly elders need to show the KG animals? Surely any experienced kohen knows what goats look like. What specific identification is the preparation teaching?

argumentFlow.label: Abaye: mishna follows Rabbanan throughout
argumentFlow.speaker: Abaye
argumentFlow.text: The entire mishna about the KG's loaf portion follows the Rabbanan. The varying 4-or-5 count reflects different mishmar configurations, not different positions within the mishna.

argumentFlow.label: Rava: mishna follows Rabbi throughout
argumentFlow.speaker: Rava
argumentFlow.text: Rava counters: the entire mishna is Rabbi's view. The KG always takes half, and the varying count (4 or 5) is explained differently - by whether a delayed or early mishmar is present.

argumentFlow.label: Remaining contradiction left open
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A contradiction in the mishna about whether the KG always gets five loaves cannot be fully resolved by either Abaye or Rava. The Gemara leaves it as a difficulty (teiku).

argumentFlow.label: New mishna: elders hand KG to priestly elders
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: The elders of Beit Din hand the KG to the elders of the priesthood on the 3rd of Tishrei (seven days before Yom Kippur). The priestly elders will instruct him in the Yom Kippur service and have him practice.

argumentFlow.label: Animals paraded before the KG for visual recognition
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: All the animals to be sacrificed on Yom Kippur are paraded before the KG so he can recognize them by sight. He is specifically shown the goats of the musaf offering to know them when he draws the lots.

argumentFlow.label: Why not goats from the lottery?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A question is raised: if he sees the goats in the parade, won't he recognize them and deliberately choose the 'la-Shem' goat from the lot? The Gemara explains why this foreknowledge is not considered manipulation.

sourceRefs.note: Why showing the goats is not problematic

concepts.def: Seven days - the period of KG preparation before Yom Kippur (from 3 Tishrei to 9 Tishrei). During these days the KG is isolated, trained, and kept in a state of ritual preparedness.

requiresUnderstanding: The Yom Kippur goat lottery system
misconceptions: One might think showing the KG the goats beforehand would compromise the random lottery - the Gemara explains why it does not.

visualizableElements.desc: Tishrei calendar: 3 Tishrei (elders hand off KG) through 9 Tishrei (evening before Yom Kippur) - seven days of preparation
Animal parade sequence: all Yom Kippur sacrifices shown to KG; special focus on the two Yom Kippur goats

quizSeeds.q: What is the purpose of showing the High Priest the Yom Kippur animals during his seven-day preparation?
quizSeeds.correct: So he can recognize them by sight and be familiar with all the animals he will offer. For the goats specifically, he needs to know what they look like before drawing the lot.

quizSeeds.q: How do Abaye and Rava disagree about the Shewbread mishna?
quizSeeds.correct: Abaye: the mishna throughout follows the Rabbanan (KG takes proportionally less than half, varying 4 or 5 by mishmar count). Rava: the mishna throughout follows Rabbi (KG always takes half, varying explained differently).

sugyot.finalRuling: Shewbread count: unresolved contradiction remains. Seven-day KG preparation: the elders hand off the KG to priestly elders for instruction and practice.

glossary.en: Seven days - the KG's isolation and preparation period before Yom Kippur, beginning 3 Tishrei.

display.whats: The elders prepare the KG: reading the service order aloud, having him practice, and ensuring he is qualified. The Gemara discusses what happens when the KG was appointed improperly (as happened in the Second Temple period - Maratha bat Baytus bribed Agrippa for her betrothed's appointment). The elders also exclude goats from the animal parade since the KG's Yom Kippur offering does not include goats from the musaf. Special foods before Yom Kippur are restricted to avoid seminal emission (which would disqualify the KG from serving).
display.hint: The Second Temple-era corruption stories illustrate what can go wrong when the KG position is bought or manipulated. The Gemara uses these as contrast to the ideal preparation described in the Mishna.

learning.summary: The elders' preparation includes reading the Yom Kippur service to the KG, having him practice, and ensuring his qualifications. A Gemara digression describes the corrupted Second Temple KG appointments (e.g., Maratha bat Baytus paying for Yehoshua ben Gamla's appointment). Foods that cause seminal emission are restricted for the KG during the seven days to prevent disqualification.
learning.ahaMoment: The contrast between the ideal (halachically correct KG selection) and the Second Temple reality (bribery and corruption) shows why such careful preparation protocols existed.
learning.memoryAnchor: Elders read the service, KG practices. Second Temple: bought appointments. Pre-Yom Kippur: avoid emission-causing foods.
learning.openQuestion: Why were goats specifically excluded from the animal parade shown to the KG? Is there a technical reason or is it simply that the KG's bulls and rams are the relevant animals for his service?

argumentFlow.label: Elders read the KG the service order daily
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna/Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Throughout the seven days, the elders of the priesthood read the Yom Kippur service order to the KG daily, have him practice the key parts, and ensure he is fluent in the service.

argumentFlow.label: Qualifications for KG: must be from the Temple
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: In the First Temple, KGs were appointed only according to Torah standards from legitimate priestly families. The Second Temple period saw this deteriorate - wealthy men bought the position.

sourceRefs.note: First vs Second Temple KG selection

argumentFlow.label: Maratha bat Baytus: bribery for KG appointment
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Maratha bat Baytus, a wealthy widow, paid Agrippa (the king) a measure (tarkeva) of dinars to have her betrothed Yehoshua ben Gamla appointed as KG. A royal official later demanded she not change money in front of him (knowing her wealth).

argumentFlow.label: Goats excluded from the animal parade
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The animal parade shown to the KG does not include goats. This is because the KG's personal Yom Kippur offerings (bull and ram) do not include goats; only the communal musaf goats would be shown.

argumentFlow.label: Pre-Yom Kippur: restrict emission-causing foods
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The elders restrict the KG's pre-Yom Kippur diet to avoid foods that cause seminal emission. Seminal emission creates ritual impurity (tum'at keri) which would disqualify the KG from performing the Yom Kippur service.

argumentFlow.label: Specific foods to avoid: fine flour and eggs
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The evening before Yom Kippur, the KG is fed fine flour and eggs in the morning to fill his digestive system, ensuring he does not need to relieve himself on Yom Kippur. But he must not eat these to excess (they cause emission).

argumentFlow.label: Mnemonic: Av'i and Avav'i - emission-causing foods
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A mnemonic (Av'i and Avav'i) helps recall which foods cause seminal emission: wine, garlic, and other foods listed elsewhere. The elders feed the KG appropriately before Yom Kippur.

concepts.def: Ritual impurity from seminal emission. A kohen with this impurity must immerse and wait until evening before performing Temple service. If it happens to the KG during the Yom Kippur preparation period, it creates a crisis.

A Second Temple KG whose appointment Maratha bat Baytus purchased from the king Agrippa. Despite the improper appointment, he is remembered for establishing Torah schools in every city and town of Israel.

requiresUnderstanding: Tum'at keri and its consequences for kohanim
The Yom Kippur goat lottery system
misconceptions: One might think the Second Temple KG appointments being corrupt means the service was invalid - the Gemara generally holds that even improperly appointed KGs performed valid service.

visualizableElements.desc: First Temple (proper selection by Torah standards) vs Second Temple (wealth-based appointments)
Elders' preparation routine: morning - read service to KG; afternoon - KG practices; evening - dietary restriction begins

quizSeeds.q: Why do the elders restrict the High Priest's diet before Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.correct: To prevent seminal emission, which causes tum'at keri. If the KG becomes tamei from emission, he cannot perform the Yom Kippur service until he immerses and waits until evening.

quizSeeds.q: Who was Maratha bat Baytus and what did she do?
quizSeeds.correct: A wealthy widow who paid King Agrippa a large sum (tarkeva of dinars) to have her betrothed Yehoshua ben Gamla appointed as High Priest - an example of the Second Temple era's corrupt appointment practices.

sugyot.finalRuling: The elders' preparation protocol is established: read the service, have the KG practice, restrict diet. Second Temple history shows what happens when this standard is violated.

glossary.en: Ritual impurity from seminal emission. Requires immersion and waiting until evening before resuming Temple service.
A half-se'ah measure (approximately 3 liters). Maratha bat Baytus paid a tarkeva of dinars (gold coins) for the KG appointment.

display.whats: The specific foods that cause seminal emission are listed and discussed. Five foods are identified: fine flour, eggs, wine, garlic, and others. The Gemara then raises a related question: what is the legal status of a kohen who has a seminal emission during the preparation period - is it attributed to illness (oness) or to his own eating? The distinction matters because an unavoidable emission (oness) might not disqualify him.
display.hint: The list of emission-causing foods is practical halacha for the KG's week of preparation. The oness question is more theoretical: if the emission was caused by normal food consumption (not excess), is it still the kohen's 'fault'?

learning.summary: Five foods that cause seminal emission are enumerated. The Gemara debates whether a kohen who emits seminal fluid because of normal food consumption is considered an oness (unavoidable) or is culpable. The distinction affects whether he can use the 'oness' defense to claim his purity was involuntary - relevant to the KG's preparation period.
learning.ahaMoment: The oness/voluntary distinction cuts to the heart of halakhic agency: does eating ordinary food and then experiencing emission count as 'forced' impurity or voluntary?
learning.memoryAnchor: Five emission foods. Oness question: if you ate normally and emitted, is it your fault? The Gemara says it depends on whether the food was itself excessive.
learning.openQuestion: Does the Talmud's list of emission-causing foods reflect medical knowledge of the period, or is it based on transmitted tradition?

argumentFlow.label: Five foods causing seminal emission
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The emission-causing foods are: (1) fine flour (soltot), (2) eggs, (3) garlic, (4) purslane (halalglogot), (5) arugula (gargir). Each stimulates seminal production according to the Talmud's physiological understanding.

argumentFlow.label: Arugula digression: also brightens eyes
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Arugula (gargir) not only causes emission but also brightens the eyes. Rav Huna: eat arugula for its eye-brightening benefits, or rub the gargir juice on the eyes.

argumentFlow.label: Oness for a kohen who eats and emits
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If a kohen eats ordinary food (even a lot of various foods) and then experiences seminal emission, is he considered to have emission by oness (unavoidable)? Or does eating freely expose him to foreseeable emission, making him responsible?

argumentFlow.label: Eating normally: treated as oness; eating emission-foods: not oness
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If the kohen ate any food in large quantity (not specific emission-foods) and emitted, it is treated as oness. But if he specifically ate one of the five emission-causing foods in their emission-causing quantities, he is considered responsible for the emission.

concepts.def: An unavoidable or involuntary act. In the context of ritual impurity: if emission was beyond the kohen's control, it may be treated differently than a voluntary or foreseeable emission.
Arugula - one of the five foods listed as causing seminal emission. Also said to have eye-brightening properties.

requiresUnderstanding: The KG's dietary restrictions during preparation
misconceptions: One might think all seminal emissions are treated the same (oness) - the Gemara distinguishes between emissions from ordinary eating (oness) and from specifically eating emission-causing foods (not oness).

visualizableElements.desc: List of five emission-causing foods with their properties: (1) fine flour, (2) eggs, (3) garlic, (4) purslane, (5) arugula. Arugula additionally brightens eyes.

quizSeeds.q: Name three of the five foods the Talmud identifies as causing seminal emission.
quizSeeds.correct: Any three from: fine flour (soltot), eggs, garlic, purslane (halaglogot), arugula (gargir).

quizSeeds.q: When is a kohen's seminal emission considered oness (involuntary) vs voluntary?
quizSeeds.correct: If he ate ordinary food in normal quantities and emitted, it is oness. If he specifically ate one of the five known emission-causing foods in their emission-triggering quantities, he is responsible - it is foreseeable.

sugyot.finalRuling: Five emission-causing foods established. Emission from these foods = not oness; emission from general overeating = oness.

glossary.en: Unavoidable/involuntary act. An oness emission does not carry the same liability as a foreseeable, avoidable one.
Arugula - one of the five emission-causing foods; also said to brighten the eyes.
Seven days - the KG's isolation and preparation period before Yom Kippur, beginning 3 Tishrei.
Ritual impurity from seminal emission. Requires immersion and waiting until evening before resuming Temple service.
A half-se'ah measure (approximately 3 liters). Maratha bat Baytus paid a tarkeva of dinars (gold coins) for the KG appointment.
Unavoidable/involuntary act. An oness emission does not carry the same liability as a foreseeable, avoidable one.
Arugula - one of the five emission-causing foods; also said to brighten the eyes.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: 'what are four or five' - explaining when the KG gets four vs five Shewbread loaves based on mishmar configurations.
Rashi: 'for the Rabbanan' (who dispute R. Yehuda in the Khalil chapter of tractate Sukkah): the incoming shift takes 6 and the outgoing shift 6; the KG waits for the closing of the gates fee.
Rashi: 'the incoming shift takes 10, needs to split, half a loaf not taken' - with 10 remaining after 2 Shavuot chalot set aside, each gets 5.
Rashi: 'for R. Yehuda, the incoming shift closes the doors on Saturday night' - a different timetable for gate-closing fees.
Rashi: 'less than half of half is not taken' - the minimum portion principle.
Rashi: 'it is all Rabbi's position, and holds like R. Yehuda' - Rava's alternative reading: entire mishna = Rabbi.
Rashi: 'and what is always five?' - Rava answers: 5 is always given when there are the two Shavuot chalot + ten regular loaves.
Rashi: 'my Lord' (Ishi) - the term of address used by the elders for the KG; a respectful form.
Rashi: 'read before him in the service order' - the elders recite the Yom Kippur service daily for the KG to learn.
Rashi: 'so he would be familiar with the animals' - the KG needs to recognize the animals on Yom Kippur by sight.
Rashi: 'in the First Temple' - proper appointments from legitimate priestly families, as the Torah requires.
Rashi: 'from the First Temple period' - they appointed only from eligible, worthy candidates.
Rashi: 'Maratha bat Baytus' - a wealthy widow; she was betrothed to Yehoshua ben Gamla and paid the king for his appointment.
Rashi: 'tarkeva' - a half-se'ah measure of dinars (gold coins). She paid this as a bribe for the KG appointment.
Rashi: 'dinars' - gold coins, the currency of the Roman period.
Rashi: 'a royal official' (dayla, from the Latin 'dialis') - the king's representative.
Rashi: 'do not change money in front of him' - he knows you are rich from before; he will find ways to extort you.
Rashi: 'feed him' - the eve of Yom Kippur morning, feed the KG fine flour and eggs to fill his digestive system.
Rashi: 'eggs' - cause emission if eaten excessively. But a moderate amount fills the digestive system without causing problems.
Rashi: 'to lubricate his intestines' - the fine foods help his digestion the next day so he does not need to relieve himself during the Yom Kippur service.
Rashi: 'to avoid causing heat' - excessive eggs cause seminal heat. So feed moderately.
Rashi: 'Av'i and Avav'i' - a mnemonic for the emission-causing foods. The specific letters point to the foods.
Rashi: 'a sign for the food' - if he ate a large quantity of any single food type, we attribute the emission to the food and he is treated as oness.
Rashi: 'but if a ba'al keri (who had emission) is not a zav' - distinguishing between emission impurity and zov impurity. They come from different physiological causes.
Rashi: 'barley grain' (se'orim) - the appearance of zov discharge is like a loose barley grain, while emission is dense like an unfertilized egg white.
Rashi: 'and semen is dense like a fertilized egg white' - the two discharges look distinctly different.
Rashi: 'Abay' and 'Abavay' as memory signs - remembering the emission-food list by initial letters.
Rashi: 'shalhin' - a type of cress (garden cress or mustard cress) - one of the emission-causing foods.
